did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a regression-oriented deep learning architecture to forecast malignancy risk scores in non-cancerous skin lesions based on dermoscopic images. It utilizes five state-of-the-art models: EfficientNetV2L with LightGBM, Inception-ResNet-v2 with a Vision Transformer (ViT), MABSCNet, TurkerNet, and Xception coupled with a Support Vector Regressor (SVR). The study was conducted on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is presented at the 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Intelligent Network Computing (ICDSINC). It proposes a novel regression-oriented deep learning architecture to forecast malignancy risk scores using the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. The study evaluates five specific models (EfficientNetV2L with LightGBM, Inception-ResNet-v2 with ViT, MABSCNet, TurkerNet, and Xception with SVR), optimizes them using Mean Squared Error loss, and reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 94% accuracy). The presence of an Abstract, Methods, Results, and original experimental data confirms it is a research paper, not a review.

title: Predictive Analysis of Malignant Risk Scoring in Non-Cancerous Skin Diseases using Deep Learning
journal: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Intelligent Network Computing (ICDSINC)
brief_summary: This study proposes a regression-oriented deep learning architecture to forecast malignancy risk scores in non-cancerous skin lesions using dermoscopic images and patient metadata. Utilizing the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, the research evaluates five state-of-the-art models: EfficientNetV2L with LightGBM, Inception-ResNet-v2 with a Vision Transformer, MABSCNet, TurkerNet, and Xception coupled with a Support Vector Regressor. Each model was optimized using Mean Squared Error loss to produce continuous malignancy risk scores, with Explainable AI techniques implemented for interpretability. The experimental results demonstrate that the presented models can effectively differentiate between benign lesions and those with early malignant-like appearance. The best-performing model achieved an accuracy rate of 94% in skin cancer prediction. This approach offers a non-invasive and scalable solution for the early detection of malignant transformation in skin diseases.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the six diagnostic classes in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset used for evaluation; grouped into the 'benign' category for the binary regression task.
One of the six diagnostic classes in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset used for evaluation; grouped into the 'benign' category for the binary regression task.
One of the six diagnostic classes in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset used for evaluation; grouped into the 'benign' category for the binary regression task.
One of the six diagnostic classes in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset used for evaluation; grouped into the 'malignant' category for the binary regression task.
One of the six diagnostic classes in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset used for evaluation; grouped into the 'malignant' category for the binary regression task.
One of the six diagnostic classes in the PAD-UFES-20 dataset used for evaluation; grouped into the 'malignant' category for the binary regression task.

all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a regression-oriented deep learning architecture to forecast continuous malignancy risk scores in non-cancerous skin lesions. Models were optimized using Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss to produce continuous scores scaled to the [0, 1] range. A binary decision threshold of 0.5 was applied post-prediction for interpretation (Likely Non-Malignant vs. Possibly Malignant), but the primary task is regression of the risk score.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A public dataset obtained from a single Brazilian teledermatology service between 2013 and 2019. It contains clinical skin lesion images and metadata (age, sex, anatomical site, Fitzpatrick skin type, lesion size, evolutionary history, diagnostic confirmation). The dataset is skewed, with the majority of patients having only one image.

use.training: 70% of the dataset was used for training via patient-wise stratified sampling.
use.validation: 15% of the dataset was used for validation via patient-wise stratified sampling.
use.testing: 15% of the dataset was used for testing via patient-wise stratified sampling.
use.other: The dataset was reduced to a binary system (benign vs. malignant) for the regression task. 911 image-metadata pairs remained after preprocessing and quality filtering.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used in the project to evaluate model performance.
Used as the primary regression performance metric and loss function to measure the average squared difference between predicted malignancy scores and actual labels (0 for benign, 1 for malignant).
Used to interpret continuous regression scores, classifying lesions as 'Likely Non-Malignant' (score < 0.5) or 'Possibly Malignant' (score >= 0.5).

statistical_methods_used.method: Perturbation experiments with localized image noise
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Applied to test model robustness and sensitivity against noise introduced to local regions of the image.
Used to evaluate and compare the performance of the different deep learning models in distinguishing malignant from benign lesions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper. The study utilized regression-oriented deep learning models optimized with Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss and evaluated performance using metrics such as accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, and AUROC, but did not perform formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA) with p-values or significance levels.

PAD-UFES-20.evaluation_setting: Five-fold cross-validation and perturbation experiments with localized image noise
PAD-UFES-20.notes: Xception + SVR was identified as the best-performing model with Specificity of 0.7752, Sensitivity of 0.7521, and AUROC of 0.8318. MABSCNet and TurkerNet showed poor discriminative capability with AUROC values of 0.6765 and 0.6528 respectively. EfficientNetV2L + LightGBM and Inception-ResNet-v2 + ViT provided identical performance metrics (Specificity: 0.7661, Sensitivity: 0.7397, AUROC: 0.8290). For benign lesions, EfficientNetV2L + LightGBM generated the lowest risk score (0.0822). For malignant lesions, all models produced perfect predictions (score 1.000, MSE 0.00) except EfficientNetV2L + LightGBM which had an MSE of 0.9335 on a specific malignant sample.

architecture_used.main_model: Five models were utilized: EfficientNetV2L with LightGBM, Inception-ResNet-v2 with Vision Transformer (ViT), MABSCNet, TurkerNet, and Xception coupled with Support Vector Regressor (SVR).
architecture_used.pretraining: Models were pretrained on ImageNet (implied by normalization using ImageNet mean and standard deviations).

architecture_details.EfficientNetV2L: Used for feature extraction, fused with structured metadata using a LightGBM regressor.
architecture_details.Inception-ResNet-v2: Combined with a Vision Transformer (ViT) to capture both global and local dependencies.
architecture_details.MABSCNet: Employed multi-attention CNN and transformer blocks to scrutinize minute details in texture.
architecture_details.TurkerNet: A lightweight CNN designed for low-resource deployment.
architecture_details.Xception: Embeddings were inputted into a Support Vector Regressor (SVR) to generate malignancy scores.

training_details.data_augmentation: Random rotations, horizontal and vertical flips, changes in brightness and contrast, and zooming (especially for malignant cases).

classification_or_output_head.details: Predicts a continuous malignancy risk score scaled to the [0, 1] range. A threshold of 0.5 was used for interpretation (Likely Non-Malignant vs. Possibly Malignant).

preprocessing_techniques: Image normalization using ImageNet mean and standard deviations
Clinical metadata preprocessing: substituting missing numeric values with the median
Clinical metadata preprocessing: one-hot encoding of categorical features (e.g., sex, anatomical site)
Manual inspection and removal of outliers and records with uncertain entries
Quality filtering of the dataset (reducing 2,298 images to 911 image-metadata pairs)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as a geometric transform to enhance generalization, especially for malignant cases.
Applied as geometric transforms to counter dataset imbalance and enhance generalization.
Applied as color/intensity transforms to enhance generalization.
Applied specifically for malignant cases to counter dataset imbalance and enhance generalization.
Synthetic noise (RGB noise) was applied to local regions of the image to test model robustness and sensitivity, particularly for malignant cases.

conclusions: The study proposes a regression-oriented deep learning architecture that effectively forecasts malignancy risk scores in non-cancerous skin lesions using dermoscopic images and patient metadata.
The presented models can differentiate between benign lesions and those with early malignant-like appearance under simulated progression non-invasively.
Xception coupled with a Support Vector Regressor (SVR) was identified as the best-performing model, achieving a specificity of 0.7752, sensitivity of 0.7521, and an AUROC of 0.8318.
The best-performing model achieved an accuracy rate of 94% in skin cancer prediction, which is considered very effective for early detection and increasing patient survival rates.
EfficientNetV2L with LightGBM and Inception-ResNet-v2 with Vision Transformer (ViT) provided identical performance metrics (Specificity: 0.7661, Sensitivity: 0.7397, AUROC: 0.8290).
MABSCNet and TurkerNet demonstrated poor discriminative capability with AUROC values of 0.6765 and 0.6528, respectively.
Explainable AI (XAI) techniques were implemented to improve model understandability and classification performance.
The approach offers a non-invasive and scalable solution for the early detection of malignant transformation in skin diseases.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single dataset (PAD-UFES-20) from a Brazilian teledermatology service, raising concerns about external validity and demographic bias.
The dataset is significantly imbalanced, with the majority of patients having only one image, and the final analysis was restricted to 911 image-metadata pairs after quality filtering.

The primary task is regression of a continuous malignancy risk score, but performance is evaluated using binary classification metrics (Accuracy, Specificity, Sensitivity, AUROC) via a post-hoc threshold of 0.5, which may obscure regression-specific errors.
The best-performing model (Xception + SVR) achieved an AUROC of only 0.8318, indicating moderate discriminative ability rather than high clinical-grade accuracy.
Two models (MABSCNet and TurkerNet) demonstrated poor discriminative capability with AUROCs of 0.6765 and 0.6528, respectively, suggesting instability in model selection.

The paper reports an accuracy of 94% for the best model, but this metric is derived from the binary thresholded output of a regression model, which can be misleading if the threshold is not clinically validated.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to compare model performances, limiting the statistical rigor of the conclusions.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets, which is critical for assessing generalizability in dermatological AI applications.

Data leakage risks exist due to the use of patient-wise stratified sampling without explicit confirmation that no patient data appeared in both training and testing sets, although patient-wise splitting mitigates this risk.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, a public collection of dermoscopic images and patient metadata from a Brazilian teledermatology service. The original dataset of 2,298 images was reduced to 911 image-metadata pairs after quality filtering and removal of outliers. The dataset was split into training (70%), validation (15%), and testing (15%) subsets using patient-wise stratified sampling. Five deep learning regression models were trained and evaluated on this single dataset. No external or cross-dataset validation was performed.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The study did not perform cross-dataset validation. All experiments were conducted on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. The authors acknowledge that the dataset comes from a single Brazilian teledermatology service, which limits external validity.

training.counts: 70% of the filtered dataset (approx. 638 samples based on 911 total)
training.notes: Used for model fitting. The dataset was reduced to 911 image-metadata pairs after preprocessing. Patient-wise stratified sampling was used to maintain class distribution.

validation.counts: 15% of the filtered dataset (approx. 137 samples based on 911 total)
validation.notes: Used for model selection/tuning. Patient-wise stratified sampling was used.

testing.counts: 15% of the filtered dataset (approx. 136 samples based on 911 total)
testing.notes: Used for final performance evaluation. Patient-wise stratified sampling was used to avoid patient-level data leakage.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: Used for normalization (mean and standard deviations) and likely for pretraining weights for the CNN backbones (EfficientNetV2L, Inception-ResNet-v2, MABSCNet, TurkerNet, Xception), as is standard for these architectures, though specific weight loading details are not explicitly detailed beyond normalization.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Data augmentation techniques including random rotations, horizontal/vertical flips, brightness/contrast changes, and zooming were applied to the training data to counter class imbalance and enhance generalization. Perturbation experiments with localized RGB noise were also applied to test robustness.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: 70% training, 15% validation, 15% testing. Split was performed via patient-wise stratified sampling to maintain class distribution and avoid patient-level data leakage.
evaluation_protocol.cross_validation_details: Five-fold cross-validation was mentioned as being used in the project, but the primary evaluation protocol described is the fixed 70/15/15 split. It is ambiguous if the 5-fold CV was an additional internal validation step or the primary method, but the results reported (Accuracy, Specificity, Sensitivity, AUROC) appear to be from the final test set or aggregated across the split.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used. The study is limited to the single PAD-UFES-20 dataset.

dataset_level_results.notes: Best performing model. Accuracy of 94% was derived from binary thresholding (0.5) of the regression output. AUROC of 0.8318 indicates moderate discriminative ability.
Identical classification metrics to Inception-ResNet-v2 + ViT. Note: MSE for malignant lesions was high (0.9335) for one sample, while benign MSE was low.
Identical classification metrics to EfficientNetV2L + LightGBM.

generalizability_notes: The study is limited to a single dataset (PAD-UFES-20) from a single Brazilian teledermatology service, raising concerns about external validity and demographic bias.
The dataset is significantly imbalanced, with the majority of patients having only one image.
The final analysis was restricted to 911 image-metadata pairs after quality filtering, reducing sample size.
No external validation on independent datasets was performed, which is critical for assessing generalizability in dermatological AI applications.

Data leakage risks exist due to the use of patient-wise stratified sampling without explicit confirmation that no patient data appeared in both training and testing sets, although patient-wise splitting mitigates this risk.
information_not_reported: Exact number of samples in training, validation, and testing sets (only percentages are given).
Specific fold details for the mentioned five-fold cross-validation (whether it was separate from the 70/15/15 split).
Confidence intervals for the reported metrics.
P-values for statistical comparisons between models.
